title: Prologue & Episode I: The Plague
paragraphs: CHARACTERS: OEDIPUS, King of Thebes. A PRIEST. CREON, Oedipus's brother-in-law. TEIRESIAS, the blind prophet. JOCASTA, wife of Oedipus, formerly wife of Laius. A MESSENGER from Corinth. A SHEPHERD. A SECOND MESSENGER. CHORUS of Theban Elders.
SCENE: Thebes. Before the palace of OEDIPUS.
PROLOGUE
(Before the palace. Suppliants are gathered with branches. The PRIEST addresses OEDIPUS.)

PRIEST: O Oedipus, mightiest of rulers, look upon this company of suppliants, who cluster at your altar. We groan beneath the cloud of plague. The city is sick. A blight upon the blossoming of the land, a blight upon the herds, upon the women laboring. The god of fire, terrible plague, swoops down upon the city and devours her. Not men die as men die, but the whole city perishes.

OEDIPUS: My children, I know well what brings you here, and how you suffer. But know that I have already sent Creon to the house of Phoebus to ask what word or deed of mine may yet deliver you. He should have returned by now.
(CREON enters.)
OEDIPUS: What word does Apollo bring?
CREON: The news is good—if sorrow takes a happy turn. Apollo commands us to drive out the land's pollution, which harbors here. If unrelieved it will devour the city.
OEDIPUS: What kind of pollution? How shall we purge it?

CREON: By banishing a man, or by paying blood for blood. For it is blood that brings the tempest on our state. The god commands us: punish the murderers of Laius—your predecessor, the king before you.
OEDIPUS: Where is this murderer? How shall I find him, when the trail is cold?
CREON: He said: seek in this land. That which is not sought cannot be found.
EPISODE I

OEDIPUS: (to the gathered citizens) I shall find this murderer! I promise you. And I proclaim: if any man knows who slew Laius, he must tell me now. If he is afraid, let him know: the informer will receive no punishment. If anyone knows the killer to be a foreigner, let him speak. If anyone knows the killer to be himself—let him confess, and be driven out, and that is all.
CHORUS: (singing) King, we do not know who killed the king. The prophet Teiresias alone might know—he whom the god Apollo has made to see the things which others cannot see.
OEDIPUS: I have already sent for him. I will question him.
(TEIRESIAS enters, led by a boy. He is old and blind.)
OEDIPUS: Teiresias—prophet who reads the signs of heaven—can you see who killed Laius?
TEIRESIAS: (after a long pause) Let me go home. It will be easier for us both if I leave without speaking.
OEDIPUS: You know something. You must tell me.
TEIRESIAS: You do not want to know what I know. Let me go.
OEDIPUS: (growing angry) You will speak! I command it!
TEIRESIAS: (with terrible gentleness) Very well. You are yourself the murderer you seek.
(A silence. The CHORUS stirs.)
OEDIPUS: You lie. You old blind fool—you lie.
TEIRESIAS: I was not the one who named you fool. The god named you. And the truth will come out, Oedipus. The truth will come out, as it always comes out, in its own time. I pity you.
(He is led away.)

title: Episode II–III: The Investigation
paragraphs: EPISODE II
(JOCASTA enters from the palace, finding OEDIPUS and CREON arguing.)
JOCASTA: What is this quarreling, you men? Are there not troubles enough, without adding quarrels of your own? Come, Oedipus—come, Creon—tell me what has happened.
OEDIPUS: Your brother tells me—this woman tells me—that I murdered Laius. The prophet Teiresias says the same.

JOCASTA: Then let me set your mind at rest. No man has skill in prophecy. I have a proof. An oracle once told Laius that he would die by his son's hand—the son born of me. And what happened? When the boy was born, Laius pierced his ankles and had him left on a mountain to die. And Laius died—but not by any son's hand. He was killed by robbers at a crossroads, on the road from Corinth. So much for prophecy.
OEDIPUS: (suddenly still) A crossroads. Where?
JOCASTA: At the place where three roads meet, near Phocis.
OEDIPUS: (slowly) How long ago?
JOCASTA: Just before you came to us and became king.
OEDIPUS: What did Laius look like?
JOCASTA: (describing him) He was not unlike you in appearance.
(A long pause.)

OEDIPUS: I think—I think perhaps the curse has fallen on me from my own lips. At a place where three roads met—(He covers his face.) —I killed a man. I did not know who he was. I did not know who I was.
JOCASTA: What do you mean?
OEDIPUS: Tell me: was there one survivor from Laius's party?

JOCASTA: One man escaped. He came back here, and when he saw that you were king, he begged me to send him as far as possible from the city—to the fields and meadows. I sent him. He is still living, I believe.
OEDIPUS: Send for him. Quickly. Send for him now.
EPISODE III
(A MESSENGER arrives from Corinth.)
MESSENGER: Good news, friends! Polybus of Corinth is dead, and the citizens have sent me to invite Oedipus to return and be their king.
JOCASTA: (to OEDIPUS) You see? You feared you would kill your father Polybus, and he died of old age without your hand! Prophecies! (She laughs.)
OEDIPUS: You are right about my father—but my mother still lives, and the oracle—
MESSENGER: But, sir—Polybus was not your father.
(A silence.)
OEDIPUS: What?
MESSENGER: You were given to us—to Polybus and his wife—as a foundling child. I myself brought you to him from a shepherd on Mount Cithaeron. Your ankles were pierced—see, the scars are there still.
(JOCASTA has gone very white.)
JOCASTA: (to OEDIPUS) Stop. Do not ask anything more. I beg you—for the love you bear me—stop now.
OEDIPUS: I will not stop. I must know who I am.
JOCASTA: (with terrible grief) You are what you are. You will know soon enough. (She goes into the palace.)

title: Episode IV & Exodos: The Truth
paragraphs: EPISODE IV
(The old SHEPHERD is brought in. OEDIPUS questions him.)
OEDIPUS: Were you, in former years, a herdsman in service of Laius?
SHEPHERD: I was.
OEDIPUS: And was there a child given to you by this man here—(he indicates the Messenger)—a child that you were to dispose of?
SHEPHERD: (trembling) Why do you ask?
OEDIPUS: Answer me. Was there such a child?
SHEPHERD: (after a long silence) There was. Would to God I had died that day.
OEDIPUS: You will die today if you do not speak the truth. Whose child was it?
SHEPHERD: It was—the child was said to be the child of Laius. Your lady within—your queen—she knows better than I do. She gave the child to me. She told me to kill it. She said an oracle had—said—
OEDIPUS: That the child would kill his father.
SHEPHERD: Yes.
OEDIPUS: And you did not kill it.
SHEPHERD: I pitied it. I gave it to this man, to take far away to another country. And he took it to Corinth. And—
(He stops. He cannot continue. Everyone present understands.)

OEDIPUS: (with quiet, terrible certainty) Then I am the man. I am the one who was exposed on Cithaeron. I am the one who came to Corinth and was raised by strangers. I am the one who met a man at a crossroads and killed him—not knowing who he was. I am the one who came to Thebes and took its queen as wife—not knowing who she was. I am the pollution of this land. I am what Teiresias said I was.
(He goes into the palace. The CHORUS stands in silence.)
EXODOS
(A SECOND MESSENGER comes from the palace.)

SECOND MESSENGER: O citizens of Thebes—what you shall hear, what you shall see, will wring your hearts. Jocasta is dead. She took her own life within the palace. And Oedipus—when he found her—took the pins from her dress and drove them into his own eyes. He put out his own sight. He said: let these eyes look no more on those they should not have seen.
(OEDIPUS enters, blind, led by attendants.)
CHORUS: O dreadful sight. Of all miseries, none greater.
OEDIPUS: Light—never to see you more. Darkness upon darkness. My children—where are my children? I cannot see them.
(CREON leads in the two young daughters, ANTIGONE and ISMENE.)
OEDIPUS: (touching them) These. These little hands. These voices. Creon—I ask only one thing. Drive me from this land. Drive me to some place where no mortal voice will greet me. Let me go.
CREON: That is Apollo's command to determine, not mine.
OEDIPUS: But Apollo has already answered. The polluted man must be cast out.
CREON: Nevertheless, we must hear his word before we act.
(OEDIPUS is led inside to await the god's command.)

CHORUS: Look upon Oedipus—he who knew the famous riddle of the Sphinx, who was counted greatest of men—upon what a sea of misery he has fallen. Therefore while we wait to see the final day, call no man happy who has not crossed life's boundary free from pain.
CURTAIN.
